patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “Every time it gets cold, my fingers and toes get these painful purple-red bumps that won’t go away.” Early in the visit, volunteer: painful, itchy purple-red bumps on fingers/toes after cold exposure; swelling; warm/tender spots; they last weeks even after warming; this has happened on and off for a couple winters. If asked about severity/complications, share: skin can crack or form a small sore/ulcer; fingers feel stiff/achy during flares; fingertips may look pale or bluish when very cold before bumps; you were told blood tests showed “higher antibody levels.” Do not volunteer unless asked: worse after washing dishes/showering with cool water; increased cold sensitivity; lingering darker/bruised discoloration after flares. Express misconceptions: you worried about arthritis due to joint soreness, and infection because one spot ulcerated and was very tender. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and say you’re anxious about costs, repeated tests/specialists, long-term meds/monitoring copays, and missing work/pay for appointments.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg 1 tablet daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Frequent cold exposure (outdoor runner; symptoms worse in winter)
Raynaud phenomenon causing acral color change and pain overlapping with chilblains
Atopic dermatitis on hands causing pruritus and eczematous changes that can mimic inflammatory plaques
Nicotine vaping (vasoconstriction may worsen acral lesions and mimic vascular pathology)

required_evidence: Cold-induced acral violaceous papules/plaques with lupus features
Exclude systemic lupus activity (normal/near-normal complements, negative/low dsDNA)

medications: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO daily (may increase to 200 mg PO BID if needed/tolerated)
Clobetasol 0.05% ointment topical BID to active lesions for 2 weeks, then taper
non_pharmacological: Keep hands/feet warm (gloves, socks, hand warmers)

tool_call_sequence.description: Confirms the intended L0 benchmark outcome: correctly identifying chilblain lupus as the diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'chilblain lupus' and explains it in patient-friendly terms.
Links symptoms/lesions to cold exposure and/or damp conditions and advises cold avoidance/warming measures.
Acknowledges key differentials (e.g., idiopathic pernio, Raynaud phenomenon, small-vessel vasculitis, frostbite) and explains briefly why chilblain lupus is favored.
Screens for systemic lupus erythematosus features (e.g., photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthritis, serositis symptoms, renal symptoms) and flags need for further evaluation if present.
Assesses lesion red flags (ulceration, infection signs, severe pain, rapidly progressive necrosis) and provides escalation guidance if present.
Meets communication milestone: explain_diagnosis includes both 'chilblain lupus' and 'cold exposure'.

Discusses treatment options at a counseling level (even if not prescribing at L0), including 'hydroxychloroquine' and 'topical steroid' as typical therapies, and sets expectations that prescribing requires safety checks.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing attempt: allergy_check and contraindication_check are completed prior to prescribe_medication if the agent chooses to prescribe; failure is critical if prescribing occurs without them.

If prescribing is attempted, performs drug_interaction_check prior to prescribing (non-critical if omitted) and avoids contraindicated therapy (e.g., hydroxychloroquine with known hypersensitivity or significant retinal disease without appropriate risk discussion/monitoring plan).
Provides a clear follow-up recommendation (timeframe and what to monitor) and advises to return sooner if worsening, ulceration, infection, or systemic symptoms develop.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose chilblain lupus based on cold-induced acral inflammatory lesions and provide counseling; no treatment tool use required for L0.
must_collect: location of lesions on acral sites (fingers/toes/heels/ears)
steps: Elicit history of cold/damp exposure and temporal relationship to lesions.
Characterize lesions (site, appearance, pain/itch, ulceration) and recurrence pattern.
Screen for SLE features and other vascular/dermatologic differentials.
Provide patient education: chilblain lupus is a cold-triggered inflammatory skin manifestation; emphasize warming/avoidance of cold and smoking cessation if applicable.
Call record_diagnosis with 'chilblain lupus'.
Advise follow-up and escalation for red flags or systemic symptoms.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis plus counseling about typical treatments without prescribing (still L0-compliant).
steps: Collect key history and red flags; screen for SLE symptoms.

Discuss that management often includes cold avoidance, topical steroid for flares, and hydroxychloroquine for recurrent/persistent disease, with need for clinician oversight and safety checks before starting systemic therapy.
Record diagnosis as chilblain lupus.
Recommend follow-up and further evaluation if systemic features are present.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis with explicit differential documentation in narrative (no record_differential tool use) and safety-netting.
steps: Assess for pernio vs chilblain lupus vs Raynaud/vasculitis/frostbite based on triggers, lesion morphology, and systemic symptoms.
Explain why chilblain lupus is most likely and what would make alternatives more likely.
Record diagnosis as chilblain lupus.
Provide preventive advice and return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent chooses to prescribe despite L0; acceptable only if all required safety checks occur before prescribing and communication milestones are met.
steps: Collect history including allergies, pregnancy status/contraception considerations if relevant, comorbidities, and current medications.

Explain diagnosis (chilblain lupus) and cold exposure relationship.
Explain treatment including topical steroid and hydroxychloroquine; discuss risks/monitoring at a high level.
Record diagnosis as chilblain lupus.
If prescribing is done, prescribe medication only after safety checks; provide follow-up/monitoring plan and escalation guidance.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 41-year-old male poultry farm worker with a high-school education. You are anxious and speak vaguely unless prompted. Start by saying you’ve had a high fever with chills and a really bad worsening cough for 2 days, and you’re getting short of breath just walking across the room. Volunteer: extreme fatigue, body aches, headache. If asked, add: sore throat/runny nose at the start, chest tightness with deep breaths, small amounts of clear/yellow phlegm, nausea/poor appetite, a couple loose stools, lightheaded on standing, and decreased urination. Do NOT mention bird exposure or coop cleaning unless directly asked about animal/bird contact; if asked, initially hesitate and minimize, then admit you handled sick/dead birds and droppings without PPE and feared trouble/reporting. Hidden unless asked about severity: bluish lips during hard coughing, fast breathing at rest, can’t lie flat, racing heart, heavy OTC cold/flu med use. Misconceptions: think it’s seasonal flu or asthma flare; worry about heart because chest soreness; admit starting leftover antibiotics for “sinus.” Emphasize cost/job fears and wanting quick meds to work tomorrow. If clinician shows empathy, soften, share more details, and ask if you’ll lose your job.

nl_assertions: Agent explains the diagnosis using the term "avian influenza" and provides a clear rationale (exposure risk + compatible symptoms) without overclaiming certainty when unconfirmed.
Agent instructs on isolation (e.g., stay home, separate from others, mask use, hygiene) and emphasizes preventing transmission while awaiting results/clinical improvement.
Before prescribing, agent performs an allergy check and does not prescribe if a serious allergy to oseltamivir (or components) is identified (critical safety check).
Before prescribing, agent assesses relevant drug interactions (e.g., probenecid increasing oseltamivir levels) and documents mitigation (monitoring/dose consideration) when applicable.
Before prescribing, agent checks contraindications/precautions (notably renal impairment requiring dose adjustment; severe vomiting limiting oral intake) and adapts the plan accordingly.

Agent explains treatment using the term "oseltamivir", including timing (start ASAP), expected benefits, common adverse effects (nausea/vomiting), and red-flag symptoms warranting urgent care (worsening dyspnea, hypoxia, confusion, persistent high fever, dehydration).
Agent avoids inappropriate medications for influenza (e.g., antibiotics without bacterial indication) and avoids unsupported claims (e.g., guaranteed cure).

Agent gives brief public health guidance consistent with avian influenza suspicion (limit contacts, advise notifying local public health/occupational health per local protocol, and discuss exposure to birds/poultry).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose suspected avian influenza and start first-line antiviral therapy with appropriate safety checks and isolation counseling.
must_collect: fever and respiratory symptoms (cough, sore throat, dyspnea)
exposure to birds/poultry or sick/dead birds (including occupational exposure)
severity indicators (shortness of breath, chest pain, altered mental status)
renal disease or reduced kidney function
steps: Assess influenza-like illness symptoms and timing; assess for severe disease/need for urgent escalation.
Elicit avian exposure/travel/outbreak risk to support suspected avian influenza.
Explain diagnosis as suspected avian influenza and provide isolation instructions.
Perform allergy_check prior to prescribing (critical).
Perform drug_interaction_check and contraindication_check prior to prescribing; plan renal dose adjustment if needed.
Prescribe oseltamivir promptly (standard adult regimen twice daily for 5 days; adjust for renal impairment).
Provide counseling on side effects, adherence, and red flags/when to seek urgent care.

Check allergies, interactions, and contraindications before prescribing.
Prescribe oseltamivir with renal-adjusted frequency/dose and standard duration.
Explain isolation and treatment expectations and red flags.
